<findings>

[suggestion] [critical] Zone "Static Analysis Services" (packages-sourcevision:static-analysis-services) has catastrophic risk (score: 0.79, cohesion: 0.21, coupling: 0.79) — requires immediate architectural intervention

</findings>

<insights>

- Low cohesion (0.21) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone
- High coupling (0.79) — 5 imports target "sourcevision-analysis-core"
- The mix of constants.ts (types) with four distinct service files (archetypes, callgraph-findings, completion-reader, manifest) suggests these services share foundational type contracts but have divergent responsibilities
- High coupling (0.79) combined with low cohesion (0.21) indicates this zone acts as a cross-cutting utility layer imported widely across the codebase rather than a tightly scoped module
- Test files for three of the four services are co-located, which is a positive sign — consider adding coverage for callgraph-findings.ts which appears untested
- Cohesion 0.21 and coupling 0.79 exceed warning thresholds; the zone is pulled in by 12 imports from unit-2 alone, suggesting it may be serving as a dumping ground for shared analysis primitives rather than a focused module.
- callgraph-findings.ts appears to have no corresponding test file while its sibling services (archetypes, completion-reader, manifest) are all tested — close this coverage gap.
- Receives 12 imports from unit-2 while sending 5 back — this asymmetric bidirectional flow means unit-2 is the dominant consumer but analyzers is not purely subordinate, creating a soft circular dependency at zone level
- The 12-inbound / 5-outbound import asymmetry with unit-2 indicates analyzers acts as a shared utility layer that has grown a back-dependency on its primary consumer — extract the shared contracts into a neutral schema zone to make the relationship strictly one-directional.
- constants.ts acts as a shared type registry but is embedded inside the service zone — any zone needing only the type contracts must take a dependency on the entire static-analysis-services zone, pulling in unneeded service code as a side effect
- completion-reader.ts name implies disk or stream I/O (reading completion artifacts) rather than pure analysis transformation — mixing I/O behavior with stateless analysis services prevents hermetic unit testing of either concern
- constants.ts defines shared type contracts but is co-located with service implementations, forcing consumers to couple to the full service zone to access shared types — extract to a neutral schema or types zone with zero runtime dependencies
- completion-reader.ts introduces file-system I/O into a zone labeled as analysis services — I/O adapters and pure analysis logic are different abstraction levels and should reside in separate zones to allow hermetic testing
- src/constants.ts resides in the src/ root directory while every other file in this zone lives in src/analyzers/ — the zone silently spans two directory levels, and constants.ts is the sole src/-root occupant, making its zone membership invisible to directory-based navigation
- The zone simultaneously acts as a type registry (constants.ts), an I/O adapter (completion-reader.ts), and a set of stateless analysis transformers (archetypes, callgraph-findings, manifest) — three distinct abstraction layers coexisting in one zone; this structural triality mechanically explains why cohesion lands at 0.21 despite all files being imported together
- src/constants.ts sits in src/ root while all peer files live in src/analyzers/ — relocate to src/analyzers/constants.ts or a dedicated src/schema/ directory to make zone membership visible through directory structure alone without requiring tooling
- Three distinct abstraction layers (type registry via constants.ts, I/O adapter via completion-reader.ts, stateless transformers via archetypes/manifest/callgraph-findings) coexist in one zone and mechanically produce cohesion 0.21 — dissolve the zone by assigning each abstraction tier to its appropriate target zone rather than attempting an internal refactor
- [call graph] 159 internal calls, 1 outgoing, 66 incoming (cohesion: 0.99, coupling: 0.01)

</insights>
